title: Strategic Review: Paradisio Data Mining & Platform Evolution
description: Review current enrichment data, identify untapped fields in the full-text corpus, and propose how to surface new data in the app/platform. Adversarial/debate format welcome.

conversation.message: Review current enrichment data, identify untapped fields in the full-text corpus, and propose how to surface new data in the app/platform. Adversarial/debate format welcome.

PROJECT CONTEXT:
Paradisio is a static web directory of ~772 businesses within 5km of Puerto Viejo de Talamanca, Costa Rica. Built as a static site on GitHub Pages (Python build.py -> HTML/JSON).

RECENT COMPLETED WORK:
1. OSM cross-ref: 418 candidates found, 407 CIDs resolved via authenticated Google Maps search
2. 22 validated new businesses merged into master CSV (750 -> 772)
3. V2 Maps enrichment: ALL 715 CIDs re-crawled with signed-in Chrome, full text saved per CID (avg 3,594 chars, 2.6M total chars) instead of previous 300-char truncation
4. Full text corpus preserved in maps_enrich_v2.json

WHAT'S CURRENTLY SURFACED IN THE APP:
- Rating, address, check-in/out times, amenity chips, prices
- Subcategory (e.g. "Hotel", "Italian restaurant")
- Source badges, contact channels (phone, WhatsApp, Instagram, FB, website)
- Description from master CSV

WHAT EXISTS IN THE FULL TEXT BUT ISN'T MINED:
- Open/closed status (297 entries) and operating hours (259) — captured but not displayed
- Business descriptions / owner blurbs (paragraphs, marketing copy)
- Competitor names + ratings + prices
- Vacation rental listings with room/bath counts
- Peak hours / busy times data
- Review excerpts (Google + TripAdvisor)
- Category-specific fields: cuisine type, dietary options, price level, languages, payment methods
- Photo URLs
- Structured data (JSON-LD) from the page

CORE QUESTION:
I need your strategic assessment:
1. What high-value fields should we extract from the full text next, prioritized by user-facing impact?
2. What's the best architecture for a re-parse pipeline (category-aware parsers as recommended in the CID v2 methodology)?
3. Are there any data fields we should consider adding to the platform monetization strategy (premium listings, classifieds, advertising)?
4. What's missing from our data that we should go get (not from text but from other sources)?
5. Surface any concerns: data quality, stale data risk, competitive positioning, technical debt.

Please read the following key files (read paths relative to project root):
- docs/paradisio_status_and_ideas.md
- docs/cid_enrichment_strategy_report.md (for category-aware parsing context)
- paradisio_app/maps_enrich_v2.py (for the parser code)
- paradisio_app/build.py (for how data flows into the app)
- docs/cid_v2_extraction_methodology.md (for methodology)

Be thorough, critical, and specific. I want an adversarial/red-team perspective — poke holes in our approach.

Strategic assessment after reading the requested docs/code and checking the current v2 output shape.

Executive red-team take: the biggest opportunity is not "more fields" in the abstract. It is converting Paradisio from a contact directory into a decision and routing layer: open now, good for X, can book/message, looks legitimate, and has enough category-specific detail to choose quickly. The biggest risk is that you are currently sitting on a 2.6MB corpus but still using a single regex parser whose output model is too small. That creates a false sense of enrichment: `maps_enrich_v2.json` has full text, but `build.py` only consumes `data`, and `data` currently has only rating/phone/website/address/check-in/out/open_status/hours/amenities/prices.

Observed current state:
- `docs/paradisio_app/data/maps_enrich_v2.json`: 715 records, 715 successes, average full text length ~3,593 chars, min 95, max 6,701.
- Parsed coverage in current `data`: rating 689, phone 572, website 441, address 136, check_in 181, check_out 192, open_status 320, hours 287, amenities 711, prices 282.
- Master CSV has 772 rows. Category distribution: hotel 179, vacation_rental 145, blank 131, restaurant 123, services 81, shopping 53, tour_company 32, hostel 23, real_estate 5.
- `maps_enrich_v2.py` captures full text but does not capture links/images/jsonld despite the methodology saying those should be part of capture. It also parses during capture, which is fine for a first pass, but should not be the canonical extraction step.
- `build.py` loads only `r["data"]` by CID, so any future extraction that stays outside the `data` object is invisible to the app.

1. Highest-value fields to extract next, prioritized by user-facing impact

P0: operating availability: normalized hours + open/closed + stale timestamp.
This is the most immediately useful field for tourists and locals. You already have ~320 open_status and ~287 hours in current parse, and likely more if normalized better. Do not display raw Maps text as the product. Store structured hours as `{day, open, close, raw, confidence}` plus `open_status_raw`, `open_status_captured_at`, and `hours_confidence`. In the UI, add filters/sort: "Open now", "Open today", and category-specific display on cards. The adversarial caveat: Google open status is time-sensitive and capture-time-specific. Showing "Open" from yesterday is worse than not showing it. Display "Hours from Google, captured Jul 10" or compute open-now only from weekly hours, not captured open_status.

P0: category and subcategory normalization, including blank category repair.
131 blank categories are a strategic leak. Before fancy fields, classify those using Maps subcategory, name, description, and source URLs. User-facing impact is huge because category filters are primary navigation. Monetization impact is also high because ad inventory and premium placements need verticals. Add `category_inferred`, `category_confidence`, `subcategory_raw`, `subcategory_normalized`, and keep the original category untouched.

P1: primary decision attributes by vertical.
For lodging: check-in/out, amenities, room/property type, price snippets, booking links, pool/AC/WiFi/parking/pet-friendly/family-friendly/beachfront. For restaurants: cuisine, meal type, dietary options, menu link, accepts reservations/order links if present, price level, bar/live music indicators. For tours: activity type, duration hints, target audience, booking URL, pickup/transport indicators, safety/age constraints if visible. For services: service type, emergency/appointment/walk-in hints, languages, payment methods. For shops: store type, hours, delivery/online ordering hints. This is what makes search better than Google Maps for Puerto Viejo.

P1: business-generated description / owner blurb.
Extract paragraphs that look like business descriptions, but treat them as low-trust marketing copy. Use them to improve search and page richness, not as verified facts. Add `description_maps_raw`, `description_source`, `description_confidence`, and display only if it is materially better than `description_full`. This can also feed multilingual summaries.

P1: links with labels.
The v2 methodology called this out but the script does not save it. Link labels can expose menu, reservations, official site, booking engine, Instagram/Facebook, WhatsApp, TripAdvisor, delivery, and appointment flows. This is higher value than photo URLs because it routes transactions.

P2: review snippets and reputation summaries.
Useful, but risky. Review text can be noisy, stale, defamatory, or legally sensitive. I would not surface raw review excerpts early. Instead parse aggregate signals: review_count, rating, mentions like "vegan", "family", "slow service", "cash only" only when repeated. Store provenance and confidence. If you show snippets, keep them attributed and short.

P2: photo URLs.
Photos improve page conversion, but hotlinking Google image URLs may be brittle and policy-sensitive. Better use them first for internal QA, visual classification, and premium sales collateral. If surfaced publicly, cache only if rights/source policy is understood. For now: capture `images` with src/alt/dimensions, then decide display policy later.

P3: competitor names/prices and nearby rentals.
This is tempting but I would not lead with it. Competitor modules on a business page can undermine the business you want to monetize. It is useful internally for market intelligence, pricing, "similar nearby" recommendations, and ad targeting. Public display should be on search/category pages, not on claimed business pages unless framed as user navigation.

2. Recommended re-parse architecture

Separate the pipeline into capture, parse, validate, and publish artifacts. Right now capture and parse are coupled inside `maps_enrich_v2.py`, and `build.py` consumes the parser's limited nested object.

Proposed files/artifacts:
- `raw/maps_captures_v2.jsonl`: immutable-ish capture records: cid, business_id/name/category, captured_at, url, title, full_text, text_length, links, images, jsonld, capture_env, error.
- `paradisio_app/parsers/maps/`: parser package with a common base plus category parsers.
- `data/maps_parsed_v3.json`: canonical parsed output keyed by CID/business id, no full text required for app payload.
- `data/maps_parse_audit_v3.json`: extraction diagnostics, confidence, matched lines, parser version, warnings.
- `docs/paradisio_app/data/businesses.json`: published build artifact after merging master CSV + parsed enrichment.

Parser architecture:
- `common_parser`: rating, review count, phone, website, address, plus code, open_status_raw, weekly hours, subcategory, links, images metadata.
- `lodging_parser`: check-in/out, amenities whitelist, property type, booking price snippets, room/bath/property listing hints.
- `food_parser`: cuisine, hours, menu links, dietary options, price level, dine-in/takeout/delivery/reservations if visible.
- `retail_parser`: hours, shop type, payment/delivery hints.
- `tour_parser`: activity taxonomy, booking/contact links, duration/pickup/language hints.
- `service_parser`: service taxonomy, appointment/walk-in/emergency/languages/payment.
- `unknown_parser`: category inference and conservative common fields.

Important implementation detail: every extracted field should include confidence and provenance at least in audit output. Example: `hours: {value: [...], confidence: 0.84, source: "maps_full_text", parser: "food.v1", evidence: [line indexes]}`. Do not pollute public JSON with evidence, but keep it for QA.

Validation workflow:
- Build a gold sample of 10-20 records per major category, including failures/min-text records.
- Add parser tests that assert field extraction and non-extraction. Non-extraction tests matter because Maps UI text creates false positives.
- Track coverage by category and confidence, not just global counts.
- Diff parsed output between parser versions: added/removed/changed counts per field.
- Publish only fields above confidence thresholds; keep lower confidence in audit for manual review.

Build integration:
- Change `build.py` to consume a stable parsed enrichment file, not the capture output. Keep `maps_enrich_v2.json` as raw/capture legacy.
- Add fields to `businesses.json` only after deciding frontend use; otherwise the JSON payload will keep growing with non-product data.
- Add search tokens derived from hidden structured fields: cuisine, amenities, dietary, service type, open days. This improves discovery without forcing every field into the card UI.

3. Monetization fields worth adding

Premium listing fields should be first-class data, not bolted onto descriptions:
- `claim_status`, `claim_owner_contact`, `claimed_at`, `verified_by`, `correction_status`.
- `premium_tier`, `featured_until`, `featured_categories`, `featured_areas`, `sponsored_disclosure`.
- `cta_override`: WhatsApp, booking, menu, reservation, lead form.
- `offer`: short promotion, valid_until, terms, language variants.
- `lead_destination`: WhatsApp number/email/webhook per business.
- `analytics_summary`: page views, outbound clicks, QR scans, top referrers, last 30 days. Even static hosting can store GoatCounter exports or periodic snapshots.
- `inventory_flags`: has_rooms_now, happy_hour, live_music_today, tour_slots, job_openings, long_term_rental_available. These are not stable via Maps; they require owner submission or Instagram/event ingestion.

Classifieds/ads fields:
- For lodging/rentals: bedrooms, bathrooms, monthly price, deposit, utilities, pets, furnished, availability_date, minimum_stay, area, map roughness/privacy level.
- For jobs/gigs: pay, schedule, language requirement, start date, application channel.
- For events: date/time, venue CID, ticket price, recurring flag, source post.
- For transport/tours: route/activity, date, seats, price, pickup point.

Ad targeting fields:
- area, category, intent (`eat/stay/tour/shop/service`), open-now eligibility, rating band, contactability band, tourist/local orientation, language. But do not create a pay-to-rank system that destroys trust. Label sponsored placements clearly.

4. Missing data to go get from other sources

Owner-submitted corrections are the highest ROI missing source. Maps is broad, but businesses know their hours, WhatsApp, current offers, menus, and vacancies. Add a lightweight claim/correction form with structured fields and manual review. This feeds monetization directly.

Instagram/event ingestion is strategically important for Puerto Viejo. Maps will not tell you tonight's DJ, yoga class, pop-up dinner, surf lesson availability, or room vacancy. The flywheel in the status doc correctly points here. Start with opted-in or public business handles already in the dataset; extract event date/time/location/price/contact from posts/stories where feasible.

Menus, booking/reservation pages, and official websites. Link crawling from captured links can fetch menu PDFs/pages, services lists, room pages, and direct booking CTAs. This should be a separate polite crawler with per-domain limits, not folded into Maps capture.

Ground-truth local taxonomy. Build a Puerto Viejo-specific ontology: beaches/areas/neighborhoods, tourist intents, cuisine/service/tour types, nightlife, wellness, transport, emergency/essential services. Generic Google categories are not enough.

User behavior and conversion data. GoatCounter pageviews are live, but the strategic asset is outbound click/QR scan/contact intent by category/area/time. This tells you who to sell premium listings to and which verticals matter.

Freshness data. Add `last_seen_on_maps`, `last_owner_verified`, `last_social_activity`, `last_contact_tested`, and stale flags. A directory wins when users trust it more than a stale scrape.

5. Concerns and holes in the approach

Data quality: current address parsing is weak. The parser sets address from `^[A-Z]\d{3,}` which catches plus-code-like strings, not necessarily street addresses. The strategy report already flagged v1 taking Plus Code instead of street address. You need separate `street_address`, `plus_code`, and `address_raw` fields.

Open status risk: captured "Open"/"Closed" is only true at crawl time. If the app displays it like a live fact, users will be misled. Weekly hours are safer; captured open status is evidence, not product truth.

Amenities are likely noisy. Current coverage says amenities on 711/715 records, which is suspicious. That probably includes generic Maps UI/category terms. The build filters a small noise set, but extraction should whitelist by category and confidence rather than dumping many raw lines.

Category blanks are more damaging than they look. 131 uncategorized businesses weaken navigation, SEO, ad sales, and parser routing. Fixing category inference should happen before deep category-specific UI.

The capture does not yet match the methodology. `maps_enrich_v2.py` saves full text, but not links, images, JSON-LD, screenshots, category, or title. The methodology's value proposition depends on these. Full text alone is good, but link/image/jsonld capture would unlock high-value routing and media QA.

Static-site payload risk: embedding all businesses into `index.html` is fine at 772 records, but not if you add long descriptions, hours arrays, image URLs, review snippets, and audit metadata. Keep public `businesses.json` lean and lazy-load detail fields if needed.

Competitive positioning: if Paradisio is only "Google Maps but smaller," it loses. The winning wedge is local routing: WhatsApp-first, multilingual, QR/print bridge, classifieds, current events/offers, owner-verified corrections, and Puerto Viejo-specific filters. Do not overinvest in reproducing Maps fields that Google already owns unless they support that wedge.

Monetization trust risk: premium listings can poison the directory if users suspect pay-to-play ranking. Keep organic relevance and sponsored placement separate. Sell better CTAs, analytics, offers, claim verification, QR packs, and featured slots with disclosure rather than selling hidden ranking boosts.

Technical debt: several analysis scripts live under `paradisio_app/_*.py`, while docs/data paths are split between `paradisio_app` and `docs/paradisio_app`. This is manageable now but will become brittle. Create a small `paradisio_app/enrichment/` package and make paths/config explicit.

Recommended next 3 moves:
1. Build `parse_maps_v3.py` as an offline re-parser over existing full text, producing `maps_parsed_v3.json` and `maps_parse_audit_v3.json`. Start with common fields, normalized hours, category inference, and category-specific lodging/food fields.
2. Update capture to save links/images/title/jsonld/category going forward. Do not re-crawl immediately unless you decide link/image/jsonld capture is worth it; the existing full text is enough for parser v3.
3. Update the app with a small, high-trust product slice: Open today/Open now from normalized hours, category/subcategory repair, cuisine/service/property chips, and "last updated/captured" freshness. Avoid review snippets and competitor displays until provenance/policy is cleaner.

Bottom line: the corpus is valuable, but the platform value will come from disciplined normalization, freshness/provenance, and owner/transaction routing. Treat Maps text as a raw signal, not truth. Build a parser/audit layer before adding more UI surface area.
